themes.text: country-security"> Stand 19.07.2026 (Unverändert gültig seit: 05.06.2026) Sicherheitsstufe Sicherheitsstufe 3 (regional) Landkarte Hohes Sicherheitsrisiko Sicherheitsstufe 3 (von 4) gilt in der Region Karamoja und im Grenzgebiet zur Demokratischen Republik Kongo Sicherheitsrisiko Sicherheitsstufe 2 (von 4) gilt im Rest des Landes. Die Sicherheitslage kann sich jederzeit verändern und entwickeln. Daher empfehlen wir Ihnen: Nützen Sie unsere Auslandsservice-App sowie die kostenlose Reiseregistrierung! Informieren Sie sich über die Sicherheitslage vor Ort und überprüfen Sie diese regelmäßig! Aktuelle Hinweise

themes.heading: Aktuelle Hinweise
themes.text: Aktuelle Gesundheitslage - Ebola In der Provinz Ituri in der Demokratischen Republik Kongo sowie in Uganda wurden Ausbrüche des Ebola-Virus (Bundibugyo-Stamm) offiziell bestätigt. Die Behörden haben Notfall- und Vorsichtsmaßnahmen eingeleitet. Ebola wird durch Kontakt mit Körperflüssigkeiten infizierter Personen oder kontaminierten Gegenständen übertragen. Bitte beachten Sie die unter der Rubrik „Gesundheit“ angeführten Vorsichtsmaßnahmen. Aufgrund des anhaltenden Ebola-Ausbruchs hat das ugandische Gesundheitsministerium eine verpflichtende Gesundheitserklärung für alle nach/aus Uganda ein- und ausreisenden Personen eingeführt. Alle Reisenden sind verpflichtet, das folgende Online-Formular vor Reiseantritt auszufüllen: https://poes.health.go.ug/declare Bitte beachten Sie, dass es nach einem Aufenthalt in Uganda zu Einschränkungen bei der Weiterreise kommen kann. Einen Überblick über die Einreisebestimmungen nach einem Aufenthalt in Uganda finden Sie unter: www.iatatravelcentre.com

themes.heading: Sicherheit & Kriminalität
themes.text: Hohes Sicherheitsrisiko in einem bestimmten Gebiet (Sicherheitsstufe 3) Von Reisen in die Grenzgebiete zur Demokratischen Republik Kongo sowie in der Region Karamoja wird abgeraten. Gelegentlich kommt es zu Flüchtlingsströmen aus der Demokratischen Republik Kongo in den Westen und Südwesten Ugandas und aus dem Südsudan in den Norden Ugandas. Gefechte zwischen kongolesischer Armee und Rebellen bzw. ugandischer Armee und Rebellen und Überfälle von Rebellen auf ugandischem Staatsgebiet sind auch in unmittelbarer Grenznähe zur DRK nicht auszuschließen. Besuche der ugandischen Nationalparks in diesem Gebiet sind jedoch mit Rangern möglich (Uganda Tourism Board). In der Region Karamoja ereignen sich aus unterschiedlichen Gründen immer wieder bewaffnete Auseinandersetzungen. Sicherheitsrisiko (Sicherheitsstufe 2) Meiden Sie Menschenansammlungen, insbesondere in der Nähe von Regierungseinrichtungen (Ministerien, Gerichten, Polizeistationen), stark frequentierten Einrichtungen wie Shopping Malls, internationalen Hotels und Großveranstaltungen. Von Nachtfahrten, insbesondere Überland, wird generell abgeraten. Wegstrecken sollten vor allem nachts keinesfalls zu Fuß oder mit Motoradtaxis zurückgelegt werden. Tragen Sie keine größeren Bargeldbeträge oder Wertgegenstände mit sich, halten sie die Fenster und Türen des PKWs verschlossen bleiben, parken Sie nur auf bewachten Parkplätzen! Allgemeine Notrufnummer: 999

themes.heading: Einreise & Ausreise
themes.text: Visumpflicht: Ja Visum erhältlich: Online - entweder nur für Uganda oder Touristen für Uganda, Kenia und Ruanda „East Africa Tourist Visa“ (mehrmalige Einreise mit Gültigkeit von 90 Tagen). Die Bestätigung ist bei der Einreise vorzuweisen. Reisedokumente: Reisepass Passgültigkeit: Mindestens 6 Monate bei der Einreise eAusweise: Elektronische Ausweise (ID Austria) werden derzeit ausschließlich in Österreich akzeptiert Cremefarbiger Notpass: Wird akzeptiert Konventions- oder Fremdenpass: Wenden Sie sich bitte an die weiter unten angeführte „ausländische Vertretung in Österreich“. Sonstiges: Ein Gelbfiebernachweis ist für alle Einreisende ab dem Alter von einem Jahr vorgeschrieben, der bereits bei der Visabeantragung elektronisch hochgeladen werden muss. Reisende müssen ihre Wiederausreise (Rückflug- oder Weiterreiseticket) sowie genügend Geldmittel für den Aufenthalt nachweisen können. Minderjährige: Für Minderjährige (bis 18 Jahre), die ohne Begleitung der gesetzlichen Vertreter oder mit nur einem gesetzlichen Vertreter verreisen, wird – zusätzlich zum eigenen Reisepass – auch eine Einverständniserklärung für die Reise benötigt. Dieser Vollmacht sollte eine Kopie der Geburtsurkunde des bzw. der Minderjährigen sowie eine Kopie der Reisepässe der gesetzlichen Vertreter angeschlossen sein. Bei unterschiedlichen Nachnamen empfiehlt sich auch die Mitnahme der Heiratsurkunde der Eltern. Muster für eine Einverständniserklärung finden Sie auf der Seite des ÖAMTC und des ARBÖ. Diebstahl/Verlust des Reisedokuments: Von der Verwendung gestohlener oder verlorener und wieder gefundener Reisedokumente wird abgeraten, auch wenn die Anzeige bei der zuständigen Behörde bereits widerrufen wurde, da Probleme an der Grenze bis zur Einreiseverweigerung nicht ausgeschlossen werden können. Inhaber von in Österreich ausgestellten Fremden- und Konventionsreisepässen müssen sich bei der für das Zielland ihrer Reise zuständigen Botschaft, bezüglich der für sie geltenden Einreisebestimmungen, informieren.

themes.heading: Einfuhr & Ausfuhr
themes.text: Die Ein- und Ausfuhr der Landeswährung ist untersagt. Die Ein- und Ausfuhr von Fremdwährung unterliegt keiner Beschränkung, ist aber deklarationspflichtig. Bei Ausfuhr darf nicht mehr Fremdwährung ausgeführt werden als bei der Einfuhr angezeigt wurde. Es empfiehlt sich die Mitnahme von Euro, Pfund Sterling oder US-Dollar. Kreditkarten können in vielen Hotels und bei Fluggesellschaften verwendet werden, das Abheben von Bargeld mittels Kreditkarte ist bei den meisten Banken möglich. Eine Sondergenehmigung für den Export von Jagdtrophäen ist erforderlich. Nähere Auskünfte finden Sie im Travel Centre der IATA. Die angeführten Mengen und Beträge sind unverbindliche Richtangaben, rechtsverbindliche Informationen kann nur die Vertretungsbehörde dieses Landes erteilen. Bitte beachten Sie bei der Einreise nach Österreich die geltenden Einfuhrbestimmungen.

themes.heading: Gesundheit
themes.text: Krankenhäuser entsprechen nicht dem europäischen Standard. Beachten Sie folgende Ebola-Präventionsmaßnahmen: Meiden Sie Menschenansammlungen und den Kontakt mit Erkrankten sowie Verdachtsfällen, dies umfasst nicht zwingend notwendige Besuche in Gesundheitseinrichtungen und die Teilnahme an Beerdigungen Waschen Sie sich häufig die Hände mit Seife und Handdesinfektionsmitteln Folgen Sie den Anweisungen der lokalen Gesundheitsbehörden und halten Sie sich über die Twitter-Konten des ugandischen Gesundheitsministeriums (@MinofHealthUG) und des örtlichen WHO-Büros (@WHOUganda) regelmäßig über die Entwicklung des Ausbruchs auf dem Laufenden Verzehren oder berühren Sie keine Wildtiere und deren Produkte Bei Auftreten von Symptomen, die auf Ebola hindeuten, sollte mit einem Arzt telefonischer Kontakt aufgenommen werden. Die ugandische Regierung hat zudem eine Telefonnummer (0800 100 066) eingerichtet, über die Verdachtsfälle gemeldet werden sollen. Hohes Malariarisiko im ganzen Land. Verwenden Sie Insektenschutz zur Vermeidung von Malaria und anderen Tropenkrankheiten. Aufgrund steigender Fallzahlen der Viruserkrankung Mpox (Affenpocken) wird zu erhöhter Vorsicht aufgerufen, sowie empfohlen, Informationen seitens der WHO und ECDC zu beachten. Ausführliche Informationen zu gängigen Infektionskrankheiten auf Reisen erhalten Sie auch beim Öffentlichen Gesundheitsportal Österreichs bzw. bei den tropenmedizinischen Instituten. Es besteht kein Sozialversicherungsabkommen mit Österreich. Es wird darauf hingewiesen, dass die Kosten einer allfälligen Krankenhausbehandlung erheblich sein können. Der Abschluss einer alle Risiken abdeckenden Reiseversicherung wird daher vor Reiseantritt dringend empfohlen. Die Mitnahme einer Reiseapotheke, die nicht nur regelmäßig benötigte Arzneimittel, sondern auch Medikamente für gängige Reiseerkrankungen beinhaltet, wird empfohlen. Wer auf bestimmte Medikamente angewiesen ist, sollte einen ausreichenden Vorrat und einen Nachweis über die ärztliche Verschreibung mitnehmen, auf der Homepage des Sozialministeriums finden Sie nähere Informationen zur Mitnahme von Medikamenten ins Ausland. Es wird empfohlen, rechtzeitig vor Reisebeginn den Hausarzt oder eine andere geeignete Einrichtung zu kontaktieren, um sich über eventuell erforderliche Reiseimpfungen zu erkundigen. Aufgrund des anhaltenden Ebola-Ausbruchs hat das ugandische Gesundheitsministerium eine verpflichtende Gesundheitserklärung für alle nach/aus Uganda ein- und ausreisenden Personen eingeführt. Alle Reisenden sind verpflichtet, das Online-Formular vor Reiseantritt über den betreffenden Link bzw. QR-Code auszufüllen (Anmerkung: bitte Link https://poes.health.go.ug/declare einfügen) Es wird empfohlen, diese Vorgabe einzuhalten, um Verzögerungen bei der Ein- und Ausreise zu vermeiden.

themes.heading: Infrastruktur
themes.text: Öffentliche Verkehrsverbindungen: Inlandsflugnetz. Die Hauptverkehrsstraßen sind teilweise in einem sehr schlechten Zustand, der Straßenverkehr ist gefährlich, besonders bei Nacht. Eine Haftpflichtversicherung muss an der Grenze abgeschlossen werden. Es gilt Linksverkehr. Mehr Informationen zur Straßenverkehrsordnung finden Sie in der Länderdatenbank des ÖAMTC.

themes.heading: Umwelt
themes.text: Es herrscht tropisches Klima. In den Regenzeiten (März-Mai bzw. September-November) kann es zu Überschwemmungen bzw. Erdrutschen kommen, die zu Beeinträchtigungen des Verkehrs führen. Uganda liegt in einer moderat erdbebengefährdeten seismischen Zone

themes.heading: Besondere Bestimmungen
themes.text: Am 30.5.2023 trat ein neues Gesetz in Kraft, das gleichgeschlechtliche Handlungen noch strenger als bisher bestraft (bis zur Todesstrafe). Das Klima gegenüber LGBTIQ+ Personen ist entsprechend aufgeheizt, es kann vermehrt zu Hetzkampagnen sowie Belästigungen und Übergriffen kommen. Rauchen im öffentlichen Bereich, außer in ausgewiesenen Zonen, sowie das Rauchen von E-Zigaretten ist verboten. Das Fotografieren militärischer und sicherheitsrelevanter Einrichtungen (z.B. Flughäfen, Brücken, Regierungsgebäude) ist verboten. Uganda ist Vertragsstaat des Washingtoner Artenschutzabkommens CITES (Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species of wild Fauna and Flora), das den internationalen Handel mit wildlebenden Tier- und Pflanzenarten und Produkten dieser Arten reguliert, was auch Souvenirs betreffen kann. Ohne entsprechende Genehmigungen können daher bestimmte Tier- und Pflanzenarten und Produkten dieser Arten weder gejagt/gefangen/gesammelt, ge- oder verkauft und exportiert werden. Das Schutzalter für sexuelle Handlungen kann gegenüber den in Österreich geltenden Bestimmungen um einige Jahre höher sein oder sogar über dem Erwachsenenalter von 18 Jahren liegen. Es können jedoch auch (beispielsweise in einzelnen Provinzen oder Regionen) unterschiedliche Bestimmungen zur Anwendung kommen.

themes.heading: 사건ㆍ사고의 유형
themes.text: ※ 우간다는 강도, 살인, 테러, 납치 등 강력범죄가 많이 발생하므로 우간다를 방문하시는 우리 국민들은 신변안전에 각별히 유의하여 주시기 바랍니다. [유괴, 살인, 강도 등 강력 범죄] ㅇ 유괴, 납치를 통해 거액의 돈을 쉽게 벌수 있다는 생각 만연 - 2018년 2월 현지인 사업가 딸을 납치하여 백만불의 몸값을 요구하는 사건 발생, 가족은 우선 20만불을 송금하였으나 피해자는 결국 살해됨 - 2018년 3월 우간다 군간부 아들을 납치하여 거액의 돈을 요구하는 사건이 발생하는 등 유사 범죄 급증 - 2019년 4월 우간다 관광지인 퀸엘리자베스 국립공원에서 외국인 관광객이 납치당하여 3만불의 몸값을 지불한 후 풀려남 ㅇ 강간, 살인 등 강력범죄 및 마취제를 사용한 범죄 증가 - 2018년 3월 엔테베공항 고속도로에서 혼자 운전중인 여성 운전자가 강간을 당한 후 살해된 사건 발생. 피해자 부검 결과 몸에서 마취제 성분 발견 - 2018년 3월 수도인근 거주 외국인 자택에 강도가 침입하는 사건 발생. 경찰 수사결과 주방에서 범인이 사용한 마취제 발견 [대중교통을 수단으로 하는 강도사건 다수 발생] ㅇ 2018년 1월 승합차(Matatu 혹은 Taxi라 함)내에서 동승한 현지인 일행에게 수만불을 강취 당하는 사건 발생. 승합차 내에서 강도사건 다수 발생 ㅇ 오토바이를 이용한 소매치기가 빈번하므로 휴대폰, 귀금속, 가방 등을 소지했을 때에는 특히 주의 필요 ※ 정차중인 차량의 창문과 차량문을 통해 물건 탈취 유의 ※ 오토바이 택시(보다보다)는 위험하니 가능한 사용 자제 [경찰 수사력 부족] ㅇ 강력사건에 경찰은 수사비용 명목으로 피해자에게 돈을 요구하고 있으나 사건 해결의 의지 및 능력부족으로 신고된 사건 중 미제 사건으로 남는 경우가 매우 많음 [마을 주민의 폭력적인 범죄자 직접 처벌(공개 처형 등) 사건 증가] ㅇ 경찰에 대한 불신 및 지방 마을에 대한 정부 개입이 약하여 범죄자가 현장에서 체포되는 경우, 경찰에 신고하지 않고 즉결 처분하는 사례 증가 - 2018년 3월 마을주민 2명을 강간 후 살해한 범인이 경찰서에서 탈주후 추가 범행 현장에서 마을 주민들에게 살해 당함 - 2018년 3월 외국인 건설업체 트럭과 우간다 교통수단인 오토바이 택시 간 교통사고로 인해 오토바이 승객이 사망하자 주변에 있던 사람들이 몰려들어 트럭 유리창을 깨고 트럭에 불을 지르는 사태 발생 - 2018년 4월 경찰이 주변에 있었음에도 불구하고 주민들에 의해 강도, 강간 범죄자가 공개 처형당한 사건 수건 발생 [빅토리아 호수 유람선 전복사고 발생] ㅇ 2018년 11월 우간다쪽 빅토리아 호수에서 유람선이 전복되어 많은 사망자 발생. 빅토리아 호수를 운행하는 여객선은 적정 구명조끼를 잘 갖추고 있지 않고 노후화 및 과적 운행 등으로 인해 위험 [정세 불안, 폭발물 테러 증가] ㅇ 공휴일 및 선거기간에 시위 및 테러 발생 증가로 다중밀집시설 이용 시 안전 유의 필요 ㅇ '25년 6월, 무뇨뇨 성당 인근 자살 폭탄 테러 및 캄팔라 카레르웨 지역 자살 폭탄 테러 발생 [전염병 유의] ㅇ 말라리아 : 말라리아 원충에 감염된 모기에 의해 발생하는 급성 열성 전염병 - 발열, 오한, 근육통, 두통 및 설사 등의 증상 발생 - 증상 발생 시 즉시 말라리아 키트 진단 및 병원 방문 후 혈액 검사 필요. 발병 초기 발견하지 못할 경우 사망에 이를 수 있음. - 모기기피제 적극 사용, 취침 시 모기향과 모기장 사용, 야간 활동 자제, 외출 시 노출 부위 최소화 등을 통해 예방 - 잠복기(8~25일)이 있기 때문에 한국 귀국 시 현지 말라리아 진단키트를 구매하여 가져갈 것을 권장 ㅇ 에볼라 : 괴질바이러스의 일종으로 주로 감염된 동물 혹은 감염자의 체액을 통해 전염 - 근육통, 두통, 인후통, 기침, 복통, 황달, 구토 및 설사, 출혈 등의 증상 발생 - 감염의심동물(특히 과일박쥐, 원숭이) 등과의 접촉금지, 동물들의 사체 또는 감염자의 체액 접촉 금지 ㅇ 수인성 질병(콜레라, 장티푸스) : 오염된 물에 의해 전달되는 질병 - 복통, 설사, 구토, 오한, 두통 등의 증상 발생 - 반드시 끓인 물이나 구매한 생수 음용하며 수돗물 사용 금지 - 익힌 음식 섭취, 채소나 과일은 생수 세척 및 껍질 제거 후 섭취

themes.heading: 유의해야 할 지역
themes.text: ㅇ 콩고민주공화국은 에볼라 환자가 계속 발생하고 있으므로, 콩고와 인접한 우간다 서부 국경 지역을 방문하는 우리 국민께서는 에볼라에 감염되지 않도록 유의 - 에볼라 감염경로는 호흡기 전파가 아닌 감염된 사람 혹은 동물의 체액, 분비물, 혈액 등에 의한 접촉에 의해 감염되므로 사람 및 동물과의 직접 접촉을 피하고 손 씻기, 안전한 음료수 마시기, 충분히 익힌 음식 섭취

themes.heading: 출입국 (in- en uitreis)
themes.text: ■ 무비자 입국 가능 여부(1회 입국시 최대 연속 체류 가능 일자) ㅇ 외교관여권 소지자 : X ㅇ 관용여권 소지자 : X ㅇ 일반여권 소지자 : X ㅇ 도착사증 발급 여부 : X (도착사증 발급 종료) ㅇ 온라인비자 발급 여부 : O (입국 시 반드시 온라인 비자 소지 필요) ■ 온라인비자 신청 방법 ㅇ 사전에 우간다 이민국 E-Visa 온라인 비자 사이트를 통해 신청 - 사이트 링크 ㅇ 일반 단기방문(최대 90일 체류) 비자 수수료 : $50 - 외교관여권, 관용여권 소지자는 수수료 면제 ㅇ 이민국에서 체류목적에 따라 연장 가능 ■ 입국시 유의사항 등 ㅇ 황열병 예방접종 카드(YELLO FEVER CARD) 반드시 소지 필요 ㅇ 500불 초과 개인 소지품의 경우, 해당 정품 영수증 소지 필수 ㅇ 2,000불 이하 물품 소지한 경우, 세관신고서 작성해야 하며 세금 납부 후 입국 가능 ㅇ 2,000불 초과 물품 소지한 경우, 통관대행업체 이용, 화물터미널에서 통관 절차 진행 ㅇ 10,000불 이상 현금 소지 시 세관 신고 - 세금 납부 시 직원에 직접 현금 납부 불가 ㅇ 다량의 의약품 반입 시, 의사 처방전 소지 필요하며 주재국 의약품청으로부터 승인 ■ 출국시 유의사항 등 ㅇ 미화 약 $8,200 이상의 외국환 반출 시 사전에 세관 신고 필요 ■ 참고자료 ㅇ 주우간다대한민국대사관 홈페이지 : https://overseas.mofa.go.kr/ug-ko/index.do ㅇ 주일본우간다대사관 : https://www.uganda-embassy.jp * 주한우간다대사관 없음 / 주일본우간다대사관에서 한국 겸임 ㅇ 우간다 이민국 비자 신청 관련 안내 : https://www.immigration.go.ug/services/visa-passes

themes.heading: 일반 문화
themes.text: ㅇ 우간다 현지에서는 간단히 손을 흔들며 인사를 하거나 악수를 하는 것이 대부분입니다. 또한 손윗사람과 인사를 할 때에는 두 손으로 인사하는 것이 예의입니다. 그러나 최근 아프리카지역에서 에볼라가 발병함에 따라 우간다 정부는 접촉 감염을 피하기 위해 악수 등 신체 접촉을 자제하도록 권고하고 있습니다.

themes.heading: 종교 관련
themes.text: ㅇ 우간다는 종교의 자유가 인정되어 있으며, 개신교 45%, 가톨릭 39%, 이슬람 14%, 기타 종교 2%의 분포를 보이고 있습니다.

themes.heading: 팁 문화
themes.text: ㅇ 우간다 현지의 팁 문화는 비교적 자유로운 편입니다. 대부분 식사를 한 후에는 1-2천 실링을 팁으로 지불합니다.

themes.heading: 주요 교통 법규 및 문화
themes.text: [운전면허] ㅇ 우리나라 운전면허증 소지 시 운전면허증 교환 절차를 통해 우간다 운전면허증을 별도 시험없이 발급 가능 ※ 단, 장기 체류 비자 소지자만 교환 가능 ㅇ 국제운전면허증 소지 시 우간다 입국일로부터 6개월 간 사용 가능 [차량운전 시 유의사항] ㅇ 차량은 우측핸들 좌측통행(한국과 반대로 운전)으로, 교차로에서 역주행으로 진입하지 않도록 주의 필요 ㅇ 주요 교통수단인 오토바이 택시(보다보다)가 매우 많으며 보통 교통 법규를 잘 지키지 않음 ㅇ 도로 표지판과 신호등이 많지 않으며, 도로에 소, 닭, 염소 등 가축이 돌아다니는 경우가 많음 ㅇ 정차 시 차량으로 접근하여 길거리 아이들이 구걸을 하거나 상인들이 물건을 판매하는 경우가 많아 사고 위험이 상존 ㅇ 회전형 교차로에서는 오른쪽 진입차량이 우선이나, 이를 지키지 않는 운전자가 많음 ㅇ 인도가 없는 곳이 많으므로 보행자가 차도로 보행하는 경우가 많음 ㅇ 도심 도로는 대부분 아스팔트 포장도로이나, 도로가 유실되거나 움푹 파인 포트홀이 많으므로 주의 필요 [대중교통] ㅇ 미니밴(Matatu 마타투 또는 Taxi라고 칭함) : 14인승의 승합차로 현지인이 많이 이용하는 대중교통 수단이나, 외국인의 경우 소매치기, 가방 갈취 등 안전상의 문제가 있음 ㅇ 일반 택시 : 스마트폰 어플 Uber 또는 Safeboda 또는 Bolt를 통해 택시 이용 가능 (편리한 이용을 위해 현지 번호 유심카드 사용 추천) ㅇ 오토바이 택시(보다보다) : 현지인이 가장 많이 이용하는 근거리 대중교통 수단으로, 사고 위험성이 매우 높기 때문에 이용 자제 필요. 과속, 신호위반, 헬멧 미착용, 운전면허 미소지 등 교통 법규를 지키지 않는 운전자가 많음.

themes.heading: 관련 사건사고 사례
themes.text: ㅇ 차량과 오토바이 택시(보다보다) 간 접촉사고가 다수 발생함 ㅇ 교통사고 발생 시 사람들이 많이 몰려들고 현지인과 충돌이 발생될 수 있으니, 사고에 연루된 경우 차에서 내리지 말고 가까운 경찰서로 일단 피신 후 사고 신고 등 조치를 취할 것을 권고 ㅇ 무장강도 및 음주운전 사고가 많이 발생하기 때문에 심야운전 및 심야통행 시에는 각별히 유의 필요

themes.heading: 기타 유의사항
themes.text: ㅇ 도로 범죄 및 사고가 많이 발생하는 미니밴(마타투) 또는 오토바이 택시(보다보다) 이용을 자제하고, 안전을 위해 일반 택시(Uber 등) 이용을 권장 ㅇ 길거리 도보 이동 시 외국인을 목표로 한 오토바이(보다보다) 날치기 범죄 피해 가능성이 높기 때문에 최대한 도보 이동 자제 및 소지품 유의 ㅇ 차량운전 시 신호체계 숙지 및 오토바이 운전자 주의 ㅇ 지방 이동 시 도로 상태가 열악하기 때문에 현지인 기사 포함 렌터카 업체를 이용하는 것을 추천. 예비 타이어, 적절한 통신 수단, 충분한 식수, 연료 등을 갖추고 있는지 확인 필요. ㅇ 우기 때 폭우가 내리면 배수가 원활하지 않기 때문에 도로가 쉽게 침수되며 복구되기까지 통행 불가

themes.heading: 기타 참고
themes.text: [한국과의 시차] ㅇ 한국보다 6시간 늦음 (예: 한국 오후 12시일 때 우간다 오전 6시) [전기 규격] ㅇ 전압 240V, 50Hz이며 한국 제품 사용이 가능하나 코드 플러그로 3구를 사용하기 때문에 3구 변환플러그 필요 (현지 구입 가능) ㅇ 정전이 자주 발생하고 장기간 지속되는 경우가 많음. [환전] ㅇ 쇼핑몰, 호텔 등에 위치한 Forex 환전소에서 환전 가능 ㅇ 일부 호텔을 제외하고는 대부분 미화 달러를 받지 않기 때문에 항상 우간다 실링을 준비하는 것이 좋음 [통신] ㅇ Airtel과 MTN이 우간다 대표 통신사로, LTE, 4G 데이터 통신 서비스 제공 ㅇ Mobile Money가 매우 발달 되어 있어, 충전 후 휴대폰으로 대부분의 상점에서 거래 가능

themes.heading: Latest update
themes.text: Still current at:19 July 2026 Updated:06 July 2026 Latest update:We advise reconsider your need to travel overall in Uganda due to the outbreak of Ebola disease, as well as the threat of terrorism and violent crime and the risk of civil unrest. Higher levels also apply in some areas. There have been confirmed cases of Marburg virus disease in Kyegegwa District, Western Uganda. If you travel from Uganda, you may be subject to entry restrictions, health screening or quarantine in your transit and travel destinations. Contact your airline directly for the latest updates and check the entry requirements of the destinations you're travelling to or transiting. The border with the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) is currently closed (see 'Health' and 'Travel'). We advise: Reconsider your need to travel to Uganda due to the outbreak of Ebola disease, as well as the threat of terrorism and violent crime and the risk of civil unrest. Higher levels apply in some areas. Download Uganda Download Africa

themes.heading: Safety
themes.text: During periods of political tension, local authorities may restrict internet and telecommunications services, and increase security measures, restrict movement without notice and impose curfews. Avoid large gatherings, monitor local media and follow the advice of authorities. Terrorism is a threat in Uganda. Attacks have caused many deaths and serious injuries. Foreigners may be targeted. Avoid large gatherings, including large-scale worship, and music and cultural festivals. Terrorists may also target hotels, bars, restaurants and other tourist areas. Uganda has increased security arrangements in public places due to terrorist attacks. Expect body, car and luggage checks when travelling. Take official warnings seriously and follow the advice of authorities. Security is usually required when gorilla trekking due to the risk of armed attack. For your safety, don't take gorilla trekking tours that cross into the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC). Petty crime and violent crime can happen. Always be alert. Don't walk in isolated areas or along dark streets at night. Be alert to the potential risks around drink spiking and methanol poisoning through consuming alcoholic drinks. See our advice on partying safely while overseas. There’s a risk of kidnapping in Uganda. The security situation in neighbouring DRC is very unstable and volatile. You may be kidnapped or caught up in violence. Reconsider your need to travel within 50 kilometres of the border with DRC (excluding the Eastern shores of Lake Albert). If you're travelling near the border, seek professional security advice. Full travel advice: Safety

themes.heading: Health
themes.text: Outbreaks of Ebola disease, Marburg virus and mpox have occurred in parts of Africa, including in Uganda. Maintain strict hygiene standards and avoid physical contact with anyone displaying symptoms. Yellow fever and malaria are common. Other insect-borne diseases can occur. Make sure your accommodation is insect proof. Use insect repellent. HIV/AIDS is a risk. Take steps to reduce your risk of exposure to the virus. Foodborne, waterborne and other infectious diseases include cholera, typhoid and hepatitis. Drink only boiled or bottled water. Avoid raw or undercooked food. Many medications are in short supply or not available. Take enough legal medicine for your trip. Medical facilities are very limited outside Kampala. If you become seriously ill or injured, you may need to be evacuated to another country. Make sure your travel insurance covers this. Full travel advice: Health

themes.heading: Local laws
themes.text: Uganda prohibits same-sex relationships, and convictions may result in severe sentences. Same-sex public displays of affection, like kissing in public places, could lead to arrest and imprisonment. Members of the LGBTQIA+ community may face discrimination, potential violence and abuse. LGBTQIA+ travellers should carefully consider the risks of travelling to Uganda. Serious crimes can attract the death penalty or corporal punishment. There are severe penalties for the possession, use or trafficking of illegal drugs. If you're convicted, you'll face a long jail sentence and heavy fines. It's illegal to take photos of certain buildings and areas or wear military-style or camouflage clothing. If you plan to take part in surrogacy arrangements in Uganda, be aware of all legal and other risks involved. For further details see ‘Surrogacy’ section below. Full travel advice: Local laws

themes.heading: Travel
themes.text: The East African Community's (EAC) Tourist Visa allows travellers multiple entries to Kenya, Rwanda and Uganda for 90 days. You can also apply for a visa from the Government of Uganda. Entry and exit conditions can change at short notice. Contact the nearest high commission or consulate of Uganda for the latest details. If you're travelling from Uganda, you may be subject to entry restrictions, health screening or quarantine in transit locations. Contact your airline directly for the latest updates and check the entry requirements of the destinations you're travelling to or transiting. The border with the DRC is currently closed. You don't need a valid yellow fever vaccination certificate to enter Uganda, but some airlines may require one when you leave and you will need to show it when you return to Australia. You will probably need cash in Uganda. However, some larger stores, restaurants and hotels, especially in tourist areas, may accept card payments. Full travel advice: Travel

themes.heading: Local contacts
themes.text: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance. There are limits to what we can do. Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas. The Australian Consulate in Kampala can provide limited consular assistance. You can get full consular assistance from the Australian High Commission in Kenya. To stay up to date with local information, follow the High Commission’s social media accounts. Full travel advice: Local contacts

themes.heading: Terrorism
themes.text: Terrorism remains a threat in Uganda. Terrorists may target tourist areas or other places frequented by foreigners and where large crowds may gather. Uganda has increased security arrangements in public places. This includes borders and at the international airport. Expect body, car and luggage checks when travelling, especially in Kampala. Terrorist attacks have resulted in fatalities and injuries, including in Kampala and Queen Elizabeth National Park in western Uganda. Possible targets for terror attacks include: large gatherings, including large-scale worship services, and music and cultural festivals tourist areas, hotels, resorts restaurants, bars, clubs, markets and shopping centres schools, places of worship, outdoor and political events airports, bus terminals, public transport and government buildings To protect yourself from terrorism: avoid visiting locations that are possible terrorist targets consider the level of security at venues you're visiting be alert in crowded places and monitor local media pay close attention to your personal security take official warnings seriously and follow official instructions Report any suspicious items or activities to police. If there’s a terrorist incident, leave the area if it’s safe to do so and follow the instructions of local authorities. Avoid the affected area afterwards as there may be further attacks. Terrorism is a threat worldwide. Terrorist attacks could occur anywhere and at any time. Be alert to possible threats, especially in public places. More information: Terrorism

themes.heading: Kidnapping
themes.text: Kidnapping occurs across the world with political, ideological, and criminal motives. Foreigners, including Australians, have been kidnapped overseas whilst travelling. Kidnaps can happen anywhere, even in places that seem safe. There’s a risk of kidnapping in Uganda, including in the capital Kampala. Kidnappings are usually criminally motivated and target the local population. The risk of kidnapping is higher in the border regions with DRC and South Sudan. If despite our advice, you travel to an area with a high risk of kidnapping, be aware that our ability to provide consular assistance in these destinations is extremely limited. The Australian Government's longstanding policy is that it doesn't make payments or concessions to kidnappers. Ransom payments to kidnappers have funded further terrorist attacks and criminal activity. Ransom payments to terrorist groups are likely to contravene Australian counter-terrorism financing laws. More information: Kidnapping

themes.heading: Demonstrations and protests
themes.text: Presidential elections in Uganda took place in January 2026. Elections can lead to protests and violence, including fatalities. During periods of political tension, local authorities may restrict internet and telecommunications services, increase security measures, restrict movement without notice and impose curfews. Political and other demonstrations can become violent without warning, causing death and injury. Protests and demonstrations often take place in market and city centre areas. Police have used tear gas, rubber bullets and live ammunition to disperse demonstrators. You should take care and avoid all demonstrations and rallies where possible. To protect yourself during periods of unrest: avoid large gatherings, political rallies and demonstrations contact your travel provider or airline to confirm your flight keep your family and friends informed about your wellbeing have supplies of water, food, medication, fuel and cash in case you need to shelter in place be prepared to change your travel plans avoid wearing clothing in colours associated with Ugandan political parties, including red, yellow, purple, and blue monitor the media avoid affected areas follow advice from local authorities More information: Protests and civil unrest

themes.heading: Border violence
themes.text: The security environment is extremely unstable in the neighbouring DRC. Cross-border attacks by rebel groups can happen without notice. You may be at risk of attack by armed bandits in border areas North and South of Lake Albert. Tourist areas, including Rwenzori Mountains and national parks, are near the border. Clashes between tribal groups can occur without warning in the Karamoja region of north-eastern Uganda. Hotspots include districts north of Kate Kyoga: Kotido Moroto Nakapiripirit Katakwi There's a risk of inter-tribal violence and armed bandits. Uncleared landmines are still a danger. Gorilla trekking Security is usually required when gorilla trekking in: Bwindi Impenetrable National Park Mgahinga Gorilla Park other parts of western Uganda You're at risk of armed attacks in those regions. Travellers have been subject to violence. The security situation can change with little warning. For your safety, don't take gorilla trekking tours that cross into the DRC. Fly rather than drive if you travel to Bwindi Impenetrable National Park or Mgahinga Gorilla Park. More information: Ugandan Wildlife Authority

themes.heading: Crime
themes.text: Pickpocketing, bag snatching and other petty crime happens, especially on public transport. Theft from vehicles stuck in heavy traffic or stopped at traffic lights is common. Armed robbery and carjacking are risks. Stay aware of your surroundings at all times. Home burglaries can turn violent. To protect yourself from crime: keep car windows up and doors locked when moving lock doors and windows at your accommodation don't walk in isolated areas or along dark streets at night hold bags and backpacks in front of you or in ways that make them harder to snatch keep your passport, bank cards and money separately never leave your bags unsupervised at a ticket office or a registration desk don’t carry large sums of money if you can avoid it.

themes.heading: Scams
themes.text: Financial scams occur in Uganda. If you fall victim to a scam, it could be dangerous to go to Uganda to try to get your money back. Fake internet friendship, dating and marriage scams operate globally, including from some African countries. Once an online friendship develops, you'll probably be asked by your 'friend' or planned marriage partner to send money. This is so the 'friend' can travel to Australia or make something else possible. Sometimes the relationship ends with very little chance of getting your money back. Other times your 'friend' or planned partner will ask you travel to Africa to meet them. If you do this, you could be kidnapped, assaulted or robbed. Business scams are common. Do no engage with unsolicited business offers or deals while travelling. Credit card fraud is also a risk. To avoid becoming a victim of a scam: check all online offers from people in Uganda, or people you don't know take legal advice before agreeing to anything don't send money to anyone in Uganda until proper checks are made get legal advice if you're the victim of a scam keep your credit card in sight when making transactions If you become a victim of a scam, don't travel to Uganda to try to get your money back. More information: Scams

themes.heading: Tours and adventure activities
themes.text: Transport and tour operators don't always follow safety and maintenance standards. This includes adventure activities, such as white-water rafting. If you plan to do a tour or adventure activity: check if your travel insurance policy covers it only use respected and registered tour operators ask about and insist on minimum safety requirements always use available safety gear, such as life jackets or seatbelts If proper safety equipment isn't available, use another provider.

themes.heading: National parks
themes.text: Some of Uganda's national parks have security risks. Many parks in western Uganda are situated within 50 kilometres of the border with DRC, where you should reconsider your need to travel. For advice on security risks, park fees and other conditions in national parks, contact the Uganda Wildlife Authority. If you plan to visit a national park: think about the security risks obey all park rules follow the advice of local authorities and park wardens use a registered tour operator

themes.heading: Extreme weather and natural disasters
themes.text: Uganda can experience extreme weather and natural disasters, including: earthquakes flooding landslides The rainy seasons are from March to May, and October to November. Flooding can cause landslides, leaving people without homes and blocking roads. To protect yourself if a natural disaster occurs: keep your passport in a safe, waterproof location closely monitor local media follow the advice of local authorities keep in touch with friends and family Register with the Global Disaster Alert and Coordination System to receive alerts on major disasters. More information: Extreme weather and natural disasters

themes.heading: Travel insurance
themes.text: Get comprehensive travel insurance before you leave and closely read the Product Disclosure Statement (PDS) for what is and isn’t covered. It must cover all medical costs overseas, including evacuation. The Australian Government will not pay for these costs. If you can’t afford travel insurance, you shouldn’t travel. This is true for everyone, even if you are healthy and fit. If you don’t have insurance, you might have to pay thousands of dollars up front for medical care. Before you travel, check your policy, make sure: you know what activities and medical care your insurance covers, including evacuations your insurance covers you for your whole trip, including any stopovers you know any exclusions – for example, you might need to pay extra for activities like motorcycling, snow sports, scuba diving or bungee jumping. More information: Travel insurance

themes.heading: Physical and mental health
themes.text: Travelling overseas can be stressful and uncertain. You may experience unfamiliar environments, travel disruptions, changes in normal routines and limited access to health care. Not taking your prescribed medications is a common cause of mental ill-health for Australians overseas. If you are concerned about your welfare, or someone else's, contact your nearest Australian embassy, high commission, or consulate. You can also call the 24-hour Consular Emergency Centre on +61 2 6261 3305. Mental health resources: Information and advice on mental illness (HealthDirect). Living with mental illness (BeyondBlue) Medicare Mental Health (the Department of Health and Aged Care) More information: General health advice Mental health support overseas

themes.heading: Ebola disease
themes.text: Several outbreaks of Ebola disease have occurred in Uganda. The World Health Organisation (WHO) declared an outbreak of Ebola disease in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) as a public health emergency of international concern and also reported cases in Uganda in May 2026. The Bundibugyo strain of Ebola has no approved vaccines or specific antiviral treatments. Ebola can be transmitted to humans through physical contact with an infectious person, infected animals or contaminated materials. Ebola is often fatal with a mortality rate of around 50 per cent. Symptoms of Ebola can include: high fever muscle pain and weakness headache sore throat vomiting and diarrhoea internal and external bleeding Seek medical advice immediately if you experience these symptoms. Maintain strict hygiene standards and avoid physical contact with anyone displaying symptoms.

themes.heading: Marburg virus disease
themes.text: Several outbreaks of Marburg virus disease (MVD) have occurred in Uganda. There have been confirmed cases of Marburg virus disease in Kyegegwa District, Western Uganda in July 2026. MVD is spread through contact with infected bodily fluids from people or animals. It is very serious and often fatal. There is no vaccine or standard treatment. If you take precautions, you are at low risk of getting MVD. MVD symptoms are similar to Ebola disease. If you develop symptoms, you should isolate yourself immediately and seek medical care. Alert healthcare providers of your recent travel to an area with an Ebola or MVD outbreak. To reduce the risk of Ebola and MVD in Uganda, you should avoid: unprotected direct contact with people suffering from an undiagnosed illness participating in burial practices that involve direct unprotected contact with the body of the deceased visiting healthcare facilities in the outbreak area for non-urgent medical care or non-medical reasons visiting traditional healers non-human primates (such as chimpanzees or gorillas) and exposure to mines or caves inhabited by fruit bats handling or eating wild animals (alive, sick or dead), including bushmeat. More information: Ebola disease Marburg virus disease

themes.heading: HIV/AIDS
themes.text: HIV/AIDS is a risk. Take precautions if you engage in activities that expose you to the virus.

themes.heading: Insect-borne diseases
themes.text: Yellow fever is widespread in Uganda. Yellow fever is a potentially fatal virus spread by mosquitoes. It's prevented by vaccination. Get vaccinated before you travel. Carry your vaccination certificate with you. Malaria is common throughout the year. Other insect-borne diseases found in Uganda include: plague chikungunya To protect yourself from disease: make sure your accommodation is insect proof use insect repellent wear long, loose, light-coloured clothing consider taking medication to prevent malaria Get medical advice if you have a fever, muscle pain, rash or severe headache.

themes.heading: Other health risks
themes.text: Waterborne, foodborne and other infectious diseases are common. These include: mpox typhoid cholera hepatitis tuberculosis meningococcal disease viral haemorrhagic fevers Serious outbreaks sometimes occur. To protect yourself from illness: drink boiled water or bottled water with sealed lids avoid ice cubes don't eat raw or undercooked food, such as salads don't swim in fresh water Get medical advice if you have a fever or diarrhoea. Ask your doctor about health risks and vaccines before you go. More information: Infectious diseases

themes.heading: Medical care
themes.text: There are very limited medical facilities outside of Kampala. Psychological and psychiatric services are limited, even in larger cities. If you become seriously ill or injured, you'll need to be evacuated to Nairobi, Kenya, or another place with better facilities. Medical evacuation can be very expensive. There’s no agreement between Australia and Uganda for free healthcare. Tourists must pay for all medical care, even if they need to stay in hospital or be evacuated. This can be very expensive. Make sure your travel insurance covers you. The Australian Government won't pay for these costs.

themes.heading: LGBTQIA+ travellers
themes.text: Uganda's law prohibits same-sex relationships, and convictions under these laws may result in severe sentences, up to the death penalty for certain cases. Same-sex public displays of affection, like kissing in public places, could lead to arrest and imprisonment. Groups, individuals, or legal entities advocating for LGBTQIA+ rights or promoting homosexuality could lead to arrest and long-term imprisonment. Members of the LGBTQIA+ community may face discrimination, potential violence and abuse. LGBTQIA+ travellers should carefully consider the risks of travelling to Uganda. More information: Advice for LGBTQIA+ travellers

themes.heading: Drugs
themes.text: There are heavy penalties for possession, use or trafficking of illegal drugs. If you're convicted of a drug offence, you'll face a long jail sentence and heavy fines. More information: Carrying or using drugs

themes.heading: Visas and border measures
themes.text: Each country or territory decides its own entry and exit rules, including health border screening measures. To find out what you need to enter a destination, ask their embassy, consulate or immigration office.

themes.heading: Visas
themes.text: You need a visa to visit Uganda. The East African Community's EAC Tourist Visa allows travellers multiple entries to Kenya, Rwanda and Uganda for 90 days. Another option is to apply for a visa from the Government of Uganda. Apply for the EAC Tourist Visa or a Ugandan visa by using Uganda's online visa application.

themes.heading: Border measures
themes.text: Check the entry requirements of the destinations you're travelling to or transiting. Ensure you review and comply with testing and health screening requirements for any transit and destination countries. Entry and exit conditions can change at short notice. The border with the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) is closed. There are limited exceptions for authorised Ebola response, humanitarian and security personnel. If you're travelling from Uganda, you may be subject to entry restrictions, health screening or quarantine in other countries. Contact your airline directly for the latest updates and check the entry requirements of the destinations you're travelling to or transiting.

themes.heading: Yellow fever vaccination
themes.text: You no longer need a valid yellow fever vaccination certificate to enter Uganda. However, some airlines may require one when you leave. You may still need to show one when you enter Australia or other countries. Find out about returning to Australia after exposure to yellow fever. More information: Countries with a risk of yellow fever

themes.heading: Lost or stolen passport
themes.text: Your passport is valuable and can be used by others to commit crimes. Don’t let anyone trick you into giving it to them. Always keep it in a safe place. If your passport is lost or stolen, tell the Australian Government: In Australia: contact the Australian Passport Information Service. Overseas: contact the nearest Australian embassy or consulate.

themes.heading: Passport with ‘X’ gender identifier
themes.text: Australian passports comply with international standards for sex and gender. If your passport has an 'X' in the sex field, we can’t guarantee your entry or transit to other countries. Ask the embassy or consulate of your destinations if they’ll accept your passport with an 'X' gender marker. Check all transit destinations too. More information: LGBTQIA+ travellers

themes.heading: Money
themes.text: The local currency is the Ugandan Shilling (UGX). You must declare all foreign currency on arrival. This covers all forms of currency, not only cash. If you don't, you may have trouble taking it when you leave. Only exchange money at commercial banks and exchange bureaus. US dollars are widely accepted. You may not be able to change US banknotes printed before 2006. You will probably need cash in Uganda. However, some larger stores, restaurants and hotels, especially in tourist areas, may accept card payments.

themes.heading: Driving permit
themes.text: To drive in Uganda, you need both: a valid Australian driver's licence (digital driver’s licences may not be accepted) an International Driving Permit (IDP) You must get your IDP before leaving Australia.

themes.heading: Road travel
themes.text: Traffic accidents are common and there's a high risk at night. Hazards include: poor road and vehicle conditions bad driving habits high speeds poor lighting The Ugandan Government sometimes closes tourist areas if there's a risk of rebel activity. If you plan to drive in Uganda: check local traffic laws and practices be alert to possible hazards, especially at night check security risks and road closures before you travel outside major centres don't travel between towns after dusk, except between Kampala and Entebbe The border between Uganda and Rwanda regularly closes for extended periods. More information: Driving or riding

themes.heading: Taxis
themes.text: Only use registered taxis and limousines due to the high risk of crime. It's best to arrange this through your hotel. Don't use unofficial taxis or hail taxis on the street. You could be robbed. App-based ride-share transport services are available in Kampala. Only use reputable rideshare services booked through official apps.

themes.heading: Public transport
themes.text: Long-distance bus travel can be dangerous. Accidents are common. Some buses travelling overnight have been robbed. They have been forced to stop by roadblocks or by criminals acting as passengers. Minibuses ('matatus') and scooter taxis (boda-boda) are usually in poor condition and badly driven. If you need to take public transport, don't use matatus and boda-bodas. If you do take a boda-boda, always wear a helmet. More information: Transport and getting around safely

themes.heading: Air travel
themes.text: DFAT doesn’t give advice about how safe different airlines or flight paths are. Safety risks and weather events may cause flight delays and cancellations. Contact your travel agent or airline to confirm your plans. Airlines and airports can restrict what you can take in your luggage. Regulations may be different to those in Australia. Check with your airline before you travel. Check Uganda's air safety profile with the Aviation Safety Network. More information: Air travel

themes.heading: Consular contacts
themes.text: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance. There are limits to what we can do. Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas. The Australian Consulate in Kampala provides limited consular services to Australians in Uganda. The consulate doesn’t issue passports. Full consular assistance is available from the Australian High Commission in Kenya. Australian Consulate, Kampala 3rd Floor, UEDCL Towers Plot 37 Nakasero Road Kampala, Uganda Phone: +256 206 300 966/ +256 776 376 817 Email: australianconsulate.ug@gmail.com By appointment only.

themes.heading: 24-hour Consular Emergency Centre
themes.text: In a consular emergency, if you can't contact an embassy, call the 24-hour Consular Emergency Centre on: +61 2 6261 3305 from overseas 1300 555 135 in Australia
